CuratorialText_x:
A master Maya potter modeled a vignette with three characters onto the body and lids of this ceremonial vessel. The main form of the vessel is composed of two identical cylindrical chambers connected by a central hollow bridge, each of which rests on two squarish supports. This style of ceramic, recognizable by a very fine reddish or gray paste fired with a reddish-black clay slip, is known from what is now northern Guatemala and Southern Campeche, Mexico. Artists who specialized in this highly polished blackware style in the 5th-6th centuries achieved great feats in gouged and incised designs, often on very thin walls or lids. Such a fine vessel would have been used on very special feasting or ritual occasion, perhaps later buried in the tomb of its owner by his or her descendants.

In this scene, a large supernatural bird known as the Principal Bird Deity (PBD) perches on one lid (which is actually a false lid, fused with the chamber), and faces a kneeling humanlike figure. A spherical chamber of a whistle is hidden inside the head of the bird, visible through an Rx radiograph image, and sounds when water is poured into the opposite chamber. The bird’s large beak and caruncle protrude from its open mouth, which connects to the interior chamber of the vessel. From the beak hangs a stylized double-headed serpent. The bird’s glittering eyes are square and marked with an L-shaped pupil, a convention that Maya artists used to depict deities with supernatural vision. The spray of feathers of the serpent-wings are modeled and incised, as are the scaley legs and talons. The bird is decked out in jade jewels: a diadem, circular earflares, and a large necklace with a central pendant.

A kneeling figure on the other removable lid presents the bird with a bundle in devotional tribute. He wears a loincloth, a feathered, jeweled headdress, nose ornaments, ear ornaments, and a necklace. He stares at the bird in reverence, stoic in his encounter with this resplendent being. The young man’s submissive position may be a trap laid to bring down the portentous bird. Despite his impending defeat, this majestic creature was revered as an avian manifestation (or a spiritual companion, way) of the god Itzamnaaj, the old celestial god. A feline or simian creature, also wearing a necklace, bridges the two chambers of the vessel and seems to climb up the side towards the two figures on top.

This vessel may be part of a series of three double-chambered blackware vessels featuring different scenes involving the PBD; one was excavated from a tomb at Calakmul, Mexico. Religious narratives from the late first millennium BCE until the colonial period feature this creature, which has a large hooked raptor beak grabbing a serpent and animate wings in the form of stylized serpents. Artists depicted the PBD descending from the sky, perching on trees, receiving offerings from humanlike figures, or even being shot by a blowgun.

Such a fine vessel would have been used on very special feasting or ritual occasions, and perhaps later buried in the tomb of its owner by his or her descendants.

In this scene, a large supernatural bird known as the Principal Bird Deity perches on one lid (which is actually a false lid, fused to the chamber) and faces a kneeling humanlike figure. A small whistle chamber is hidden inside the bird's head, visible through an X-ray image, and sounds when water is poured into the opposite chamber. The bird's large beak and fleshy growth protrude from its open mouth, which connects to the interior chamber of the vessel. Hanging from the beak is a stylized double-headed serpent. The bird's glittering eyes are square and marked with an L-shaped pupil—a convention Maya artists used to show that deities had supernatural vision. The spray of feathers on the serpent-shaped wings is modeled and carved, as are the scaly legs and talons. The bird is adorned with jade jewelry: a crown, circular ear ornaments, and a large necklace with a central pendant.

A kneeling figure on the other removable lid presents the bird with a bundle as a devotional offering. He wears a loincloth, a feathered, jeweled headdress, nose ornaments, ear ornaments, and a necklace. He stares at the bird in reverence, stoic in his encounter with this magnificent being. The young man's submissive position may actually be a trap set to bring down the ominous bird. Despite his impending defeat, this majestic creature was revered as a bird form (or a spiritual companion, called a *way*) of the god Itzamnaaj, the old celestial god. A feline or monkey-like creature, also wearing a necklace, bridges the two chambers of the vessel and seems to climb up the side toward the two figures on top.

This vessel may be part of a series of three double-chambered blackware vessels featuring different scenes involving the Principal Bird Deity; one was excavated from a tomb at Calakmul, Mexico. Religious stories from the late first millennium BCE until the colonial period feature this creature, which has a large hooked raptor beak grabbing a serpent and animated wings in the form of stylized serpents. Artists depicted the Principal Bird Deity descending from the sky, perching on trees, receiving offerings from humanlike figures, or even being shot with a blowgun.

CuratorialText_x: Ceramic drums with central, bulging sounding chambers were made in southern Peru at the turn of the first millennium. Among the most elaborately finished are those of Nasca style. They were surfaced with the many rich colors commonly used on Nasca ceramic vessels. A favored form was one in which a fat-bodied figure was worked into the shape of the instrument, the rotund body spreading out equally on all sides and the legs drawn up in the front. The figure is depicted atop the wide mouth of the drum, over which a skin would have been stretched. The image is symbolically complex; a snake emerges from under the figure's chin and a killer whale outlines each eye. The killer whales are in profile and show the "two-tone" color differentiation normally given them in Nasca depictions. A headband is wound around the head and tied to form a hornlike projection on the forehead. In back, the figure's hair is shown as serpents with long tongues.

CuratorialText_x: The three-dimensional figures of the Tolita-Tumaco area are among the most striking of Precolumbian gold objects. The present example is particularly distinguished by its fancy nose ornament, evidence that some form of headdress was once present, and by the position of the hands, which originally held objects now missing, perhaps an atlatl and darts. The feet too have been lost. Made of many pieces of high-quality sheet gold, the figure may have been clothed for special occasions. The Tolita-Tumaco style area crosses the Colombia-Ecuador border along the humid Pacific coast.

CuratorialText_x: This cylindrical drinking cup is the magnum opus of the Maya vase painter known as the Metropolitan Master. It contains one of the finest extant deity portraits from the Classic Maya corpus. The young rain god, named Chahk, poses mid-stride, lifting off his left foot and extending the right leg in front of him, gracefully pointing his toes. The underside of each leg is marked with a scale pattern, evoking a shimmering and wet aquatic creature. He wears a complex loincloth that shows the typical knotted cotton of his costuming, and the rear panel of the loincloth contorts as if the artist was referencing a fish tail. The necklace of Chahk is quite unique: the collar consists of pendant extruded eyeballs and the pectoral is in the shape of an upside-down water jar marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, with what looks like a small serpent emerging from its mouth. His other jewels on the ankles and wrists may be jade or another precious material, and his headdress is a wild tangle of watery vegetation. The typical shell earrings accent the barbel extending from the nostril and projecting off of the god’s chin. His humanlike quality is emphasized by the artists’ brushstrokes in the distinguished profile of the face, the point of the elbow, the ankle joints, and finger and toenails. In his right hand, he grasps the wooden handle of a shining stone axe, and his left hand holds an animate stone. The rain god actively engages with a giant agnathus creature, likely the representation of a witz, the spirit of a mountain, as his leg crosses in front of the upper lip while his left arm passes behind. It is as if the rain personified needs to partake in a ritual combative dance with an animate mountain to set the actions presented here in motion. The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid present on crocodilians in Maya art, a jagged tooth, and liquid or vegetation spewing forth and spilling on the ground line at Chahk’s feet. The head of the mountain is covered with the grape bunch markings that signify that it is a stony place. Most significant about the zoomorphic mountain is the character reclining on top of it: the supernatural baby jaguar. The face of the jaguar baby character is clearly supernatural and contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human-like visage. The square eye is a marker of godliness in Maya art, and the overbite with shark-like tooth also denotes that this reclining personage is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, both of which are shown with this protruding tooth at times. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to portray those of a jaguar. In fact, all signs point to this creature as an infant jaguar, known from other portrayals of the same reclining infant, and the hieroglyphic logogram found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a similar knotted hairstyle and vegetal headdress as Chahk and flails about as if fitfully searching for stability. Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night, with a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two extruded eyeballs, insect-like carapace, distended belly of a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a denizen of the Maya underworld who plays a role in this myth of the birth of the baby jaguar. He dances too, raising off his left foot and extending the right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption hovering above him. The death god wears an elaborate backrack composed of textiles, bone elements, and extruded eyeballs. The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly. He appears as a skeletal cyclops, the central eye in the form of akbal, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three extruded eyeballs crown his head, and the artist delicately rendered his insect-like hind legs and abdomen. He holds a cigar or torch, a Maya artistic convention for signifying a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of mating bioluminescence of lightning bugs. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. He pants in the rear of the dancing death god and raises up on his hind legs as if begging for a morsel of food or playing. The text comprising the eight glyph blocks that float above the baby jaguar is opaque in meaning. The owner is named as a "k’uhul chatan winik," a royal title in use during the Classic Period at certain places. The overarching theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction of life-giving rains and rotting death, the contrasts needed to produce life, represented in this case by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present as organic remains decay and when rains fall, life begins at the site of the fertilized space. All of this occurs on the top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash used by the artist on the lower portion of the vessel perhaps represents the steamy breath that emerges from caves in order to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk in all his glory emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

The baby jaguar’s face is clearly that of a god, and it contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human-like appearance. The square eye is a marker of divinity in Maya art, and the overbite with a shark-like tooth also indicates that this reclining figure is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, both of whom are sometimes shown with this protruding tooth. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to look like those of a jaguar. In fact, all signs point to this creature being an infant jaguar, known from other portrayals of the same reclining infant and from the hieroglyphic symbol found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a knotted hairstyle and a headdress of plants similar to Chahk’s, and it flails about as if searching for stability.

Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night. It has a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two protruding eyeballs, an insect-like shell, the swollen belly of a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a resident of the Maya underworld who plays a role in this myth of the baby jaguar’s birth. He also dances, lifting his left foot and extending his right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption floating above him. The death god wears an elaborate backpack made of textiles, bone elements, and protruding eyeballs.

The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly, shown as a skeletal one-eyed creature, with its central eye in the form of *akbal*, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three protruding eyeballs crown its head, and the artist delicately rendered its insect-like hind legs and abdomen. It holds a cigar or torch—a Maya artistic convention for signifying a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of the mating bioluminescence of lightning bugs. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. It pants behind the dancing death god and raises up on its hind legs as if begging for food or playing.

The text made up of eight hieroglyph blocks floating above the baby jaguar is unclear in meaning. The owner is named with a royal title, "k’uhul chatan winik," used during the Classic Period in certain places. The overarching theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction between life-giving rains and rotting death—the contrasts needed to produce life, represented here by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present because when organic matter decays and rains fall, life begins in that fertilized space. All of this occurs on top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash of color the artist used on the lower part of the vessel perhaps represents the steamy breath that emerges from caves to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk, in all his glory, emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

CuratorialText_x: Paired male and female figures are common subjects in the ceramics of the western Mexico peoples of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit. Named for the states in which the works have been discovered in quantity in deeply buried, multichambered shaft tombs, the ceramic sculptures show aspects of daily and ritual life, from ballgame representations to feasting ceremonies to deceptively simple family scenes. While subject matter was relatively consistent throughout the entire region, style distinctions existed. The figures here are in Nayarit's Ixtlán del Río style, which is characterized by thin, pliant limbs, and stout, cylindrical torsos. Gender differences center on attire and activity, but both sexes wear numerous ornaments on ears and nose. Here the male figure wears a conical hat, a shoulder cloth, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and raised drumstick in the other. His companion wears a shoulder cloth and skirt, and her right arm is draped around his shoulder.

persona1_adapted:
In western Mexico, artists from the regions of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit often made ceramic sculptures of paired men and women. These sculptures were found in deep, underground tombs with multiple rooms, called shaft tombs. The ceramics show scenes from daily life and special ceremonies. They include ballgames, feasting, and simple family moments.

Even though the subject matter was similar across the region, each area had its own style. The figures here are from the Ixtlán del Río style in Nayarit. This style is known for thin, flexible limbs and short, round bodies.

The artists showed gender differences through clothing and activities. Both men and women wear many ear and nose ornaments. In this sculpture, the man wears a cone-shaped hat, a cloth over his shoulder, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and a raised drumstick in the other. His partner wears a shoulder cloth and a skirt, and her right arm rests around his shoulder.

NameOfTheObject: Bottle in the shape of a feline
CuratorialText_x: This Wari bottle seamlessly merges the body of an anthropomorphized feline with that of a flask-like vessel. Combining in-the-round, low-relief, and painted elements, this ceramic is both visually and technically intricate. The depth of its earthy tones and its balance of volumes brilliantly illustrate the ability of Wari artists to fuse Andean mastery of color and sculpture in the art of pottery. Standing upright with its arms against its body, the feline’s posture de-emphasizes its animality. The figure stares defiantly at the viewer with its eyes wide-open, a threatening stance amplified by its upright ears, gaping fanged mouth, and dotted eyebrows. The animal’s paws are partially humanized, depicted with digits rather than claws and topped with a grey wristband. The clawed fingers grab a fantastical creature, commonly known as the "humped animal" in scholarship (Menzel 1964; Knobloch 1983), on each side (one side is damaged, perhaps from prolonged exposure to salts in the ground). The two humped animals were painted in brick red with a yellow outline on raised roundels. This color scheme is reversed on the two figures on the circumference of the bottle, whose elongated bodies fit closely the curvature of the vessel’s chamber. Geometric designs, such as dotted circles and rhombuses with appendages, complement the painted decoration of the vessel’s body. A row of orange, brick red, and grey chevrons crown the bottle’s spout, a feature common to Wari ceramics. The highly burnished orange slip (a suspension of clay mixed with water, often with added pigments) on this bottle provides a smooth and glossy background that accentuates the raised roundels and feline’s legs on the ceramic. The white highlights stand out on this surface, in particular the eyes, fangs, and claws of the multiple figures—lethal attributes that suggest that this object abounds with dangerous wildlife. By bringing together naturalistically-rendered animal features, hybrid supernatural figures, and abstract geometric motifs, this bottle invokes a complex microcosm of bodies and forms, which the viewer must examine from a distance as well as up-close. The imagery unfolds as the viewer handles, turns, and angles the vessel. This bottle comes from a looted elite burial from the Ingenio Valley in the Nasca drainage on the south coast of present-day Peru (Tello 1917; Menzel 1968). From around 600 to 1000 CE, the Wari ran an empire that expanded throughout most of the Central Andes, albeit with varying degrees of presence and control. Wari culture was born out of the interactions between the Huarpa, a local group of the central highlands in present-day Ayacucho, and the Nasca people on the South Coast. Its empire bridged multiple regions, cultures, and artistic practices. Indeed, this bottle features techniques and motifs inherited from the Nasca repertoire, from its polychromy to motifs such as the humped animal and the ventrally extended creature, but they are combined with elements taken from northern traditions, such as the modeled feline. An almost identical bottle was excavated at the site of Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru (Paredes Olvera 2016: fig. 16). Archaeologists believe that it was imported there, possibly from the Wari heartland. Whether the bottles from Wilkawain and the Ingenio Valley were both made in the same workshop and then exported to different regions remains unknown. While other Wari bottles have a similarly flattened body, this model differs in that it has to be oriented sideways in order for the viewer to face the feline, adapting the form of the container to the figure. Although it is possible that this bottle was never used and only destined to be deposited in a burial, it could have once contained liquid to be poured on the ground or ingested, perhaps maize- or red peppercorn- beer (Sayre et al. 2012). The vessel’s small size suggests that it was meant for personal devotional practice. As one held this bottle featuring multiple powerful beings in one’s hands, the viewing experience would have evolved, especially as the bottle was tilted upside-down to pour liquid from the bottle’s spout. Would this physical manipulation have prompted in the user sensations of fear and respect for these entities, or instead a sense of control over them?

NameOfTheObject: Plate with a hunting scene from the tale of Bahram Gur and Azadeh
Dimension: Height 1.62 in. (4.11 cm), Diameter 7.9 in. (20.1 cm)
CuratorialText_x: The imagery on this plate represents the earliest known depiction of a well-known episode from the story of Bahram Gur or Bahram V, the Sasanian king (r. 420 –438). The story seems to have been popular for centuries—both in Sasanian times and under the Islamic rule that followed—but was only recorded in the Shahnamah, or Book of Kings, by Firdausi in the early eleventh century A.D., centuries after this plate was created. The Shahnamah recounts how Bahram Gur, challenged to feats of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, changed a male gazelle to a female and a female to a male and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to the story of Bahram Gur because the horns of the male have been shot off with an arrow, transforming his appearance into that of a female, while the two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like those of a male gazelle. This plate was likely hammered into shape and then decorated to highlight its varied surface contours and colors, making its human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Large numbers of gold and silver vessels made during Sasanian rule in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, demonstrating an ancient Near Eastern tradition that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman world, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and prestige. These objects, presumably official state products, were presented as royal gifts to persons of importance within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.

persona1_adapted:
The images on this plate show the earliest known version of a famous story about Bahram Gur (also called Bahram V), a king who ruled from 420 to 438. The story was popular for hundreds of years—both during Bahram’s time and later under Islamic rule. But it was not written down until the early 1000s A.D., in a book called the *Shahnamah* (or *Book of Kings*) by the poet Firdausi. That is centuries after this plate was made.

The *Shahnamah* tells how Bahram Gur’s favorite musician, Azadeh, challenged him to archery tricks. He shot a male gazelle to make it look like a female, and a female to look like a male. He also pinned a gazelle’s back leg to its ear. This plate shows that story directly. The male gazelle’s horns have been shot off with an arrow, so it now looks like a female. The female gazelle has two arrows stuck in its head, making it appear to have horns like a male.

The plate was likely hammered into shape, then decorated to make its surface stand out. The human and animal figures are raised, or in **relief** (meaning they stick out from the background). Parts of the plate were covered with a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted on.

Many gold and silver vessels from the Sasanian period in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived. They show an ancient tradition from the Near East that spread west to the Greek and Roman worlds. There, precious metal vessels also became symbols of power and status. These objects were probably official royal products. They were given as gifts to important people inside and outside the Sasanian empire.

The horns of the male gazelle have been shot off with an arrow, transforming its appearance into that of a female. Meanwhile, two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like a male gazelle.

This plate was probably hammered into shape and then decorated to emphasize its varied surface contours and colors. This technique makes the human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface.

NameOfTheObject: Furniture element carved in the round with the head of a roaring lion
CuratorialText_x: This fragmentary piece takes the form of the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. During the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal complexes at Nimrud were sacked and burned, and the lion was likely blackened through exposure to fire at this time. The flat back suggests that this piece may have originally been attached to a piece of wooden furniture such as the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and incised muzzle are detailed with incised dots and the eyes are deeply carved for now-lost semiprecious stone or colored glass inlays. A pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ribs is preserved on the right side at the back of the face, perhaps to represent a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions are depicted on royal reliefs from the Assyrian palaces. Because it shares the same subject matter, this piece has been attributed to an Assyrian style. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

NameOfTheObject: Proto-Cuneiform tablet with seal impressions: administrative account of barley distribution with cylinder seal impression of a male figure, hunting dogs, and boars
CuratorialText_x: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.

persona1_adapted:
One of the most important inventions from ancient southern Mesopotamia was writing. Around 3200 B.C., people in this region developed written language. It started as simple pictures called pictographs. Over time, these pictures turned into abstract symbols known as cuneiform.

The pictographs on this tablet are called proto-cuneiform. They were drawn into soft clay using a pointed tool. Next to these pictures, small circular marks stood for numbers.

Cuneiform means "wedge-shaped." Scribes wrote it by pressing a reed pen, or stylus, with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. When the clay dried, it became a good surface for writing. If the tablet was fired, the writing became permanent.

Early writing was mainly used to record and store economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain given out by a large temple. However, early texts have no verbs, so it is hard to know for sure what they mean.

Besides the writing, this tablet also has an image from a cylinder seal. The seal was pressed into both sides and edges of the tablet before the writing was added. This seal has not survived. The image shows a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash. He is hunting or herding boars in a marsh of reeds. This figure is called the priest-king. We can identify him by his clothing and pose. Here, he appears as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.

NameOfTheObject: Cylinder seal and modern impression: hunting scene
CuratorialText_x:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene consists of two basic groupings that form a continuous overall design, unified by a stylized landscape. Above the man, a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession: cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by seal engravers expanded to include many new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas—fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples—were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which forms are cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge for the seal carver was to create a design that would keep its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface, or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression, you can see how the rolled-out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in fragments, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.

In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled across various objects made of clay. When pressed onto tablets or tablet cases, the seal's imprint served to identify the authority responsible for the document's contents—much like a signature does today. When used on sealings (lumps of clay that secured doors or the lids of storage jars), the imprints identified the owner and helped prevent unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made from durable materials, especially stone, but also metal and fired clay. Pierced through the center like a bead, these seals were also believed to have protective, or apotropaic, powers and were worn as jewelry or pinned onto clothing.

In one group, two tall trees frame a hunter grabbing an ibex (a type of wild goat). Above the man, a cuneiform inscription—a script made of wedge-shaped marks—gives the seal owner's name, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession: cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other with three trees between them. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, a time when the images used by seal carvers expanded to include many new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a connection between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and everyday ideas, central to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were brought to life in the tiny images carved onto the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the design is cut into the stone, so that when rolled, the raised parts create the impression. The challenge for the seal carver was to create a design that would remain balanced and clear whether rolled only halfway across a small surface or twice its length across a larger one. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in fragments, cylinder seals are unique in that they appear almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.

They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.

CuratorialText_x: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.

CuratorialText_x: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.

NameOfTheObject: Openwork furniture plaque with a grazing oryx in a forest of fronds
CuratorialText_x: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.

This object is part of a group of plaques showing animals and stylized plants. Stylized means the plants are shown in a simplified, decorative way, not exactly like real ones. Master carvers made these plaques using a delicate openwork technique. Openwork means the ivory is carved with holes or cut-out areas, like a lace pattern. This technique was typical of Phoenician ivory carving.

However, the style and subjects in these plaques are very similar to stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf in northern Syria. A relief is a carving that sticks out from a flat background. Because of these similarities, experts debate which tradition actually produced these fine panels.

NameOfTheObject: Figure of a man with an oryx, a monkey, and a leopard skin
CuratorialText_x: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

NameOfTheObject: Bronze statue of the emperor Trebonianus Gallus
CuratorialText_x:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.

NameOfTheObject: Marble statue of Eirene (the personification of peace)
CuratorialText_x: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.

NameOfTheObject: Wall painting on black ground: landscape, from the imperial villa at Boscotrecase
CuratorialText_x: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
YearOfTheArtwork: last decade of the 1st century BCE

persona1_adapted:
Many rich families had summer homes along the coast near Naples. One of the most luxurious belonged to Agrippa. He was a close friend of Emperor Augustus and married to Augustus’s daughter, Julia. The villa sat on a hill near the modern town of Boscotrecase, overlooking the Bay of Naples.

Workers accidentally found the villa while building a railway. It was partly dug up between 1903 and 1905. Workers removed wall paintings that still survived in four bedrooms. The Metropolitan Museum in New York took sections from three rooms. The rest went to the Archaeological Museum in Naples.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C. Inscriptions show that his son, Agrippa Postumus, became the owner in 11 B.C. The paintings were likely made during renovations that started at that time. Artists who worked for the emperor’s household painted them. These are some of the finest examples of Roman wall painting that still exist.

The so-called Black Room was one of the bedrooms. It faced south toward the mountain slope and the sea. Light came through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. This room’s decoration is a masterpiece of the “third style” of Roman wall painting. That style was popular during Augustus’s reign.

The design plays with architectural shapes. A low red base runs along the bottom of the wall. Thin white columns seem to rise from it against a black background. These almost weightless columns hold up small roofs, candle holders, tripods, and a narrow ledge that goes around the room. They are decorated like tiny jewels.

On the back wall, tiny swans perch on thin spiral lines. Swans were the bird of Apollo, the god who protected Augustus. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style designs reminded viewers that Egypt had recently been added to the Roman Empire after Cleopatra’s death in 30 B.C.

This design does not create much sense of depth or space. The black walls look flat but also seem to dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of the blackness. The walls were polished to a high shine. At night, lit by lamps, they must have looked magical.

persona3_adapted:
Along the coast near Naples, many wealthy Romans built private summer villas. One of the most luxurious must have belonged to Agrippa, a close friend of Emperor Augustus and the husband of his daughter, Julia. This villa overlooked the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. It was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905, after workers accidentally discovered it during railway construction. The wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms, while the Archaeological Museum in Naples received the rest.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C., and inscriptions found at the site indicate that his son, Agrippa Postumus, became the villa’s owner in 11 B.C. The frescoes were likely painted during renovations that began at that time. Created by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest surviving examples of Roman wall painting.

The so-called Black Room was one of a series of bedrooms facing south, toward the mountain slope and the sea. Light entered through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. This room’s decoration is both puzzling and sophisticated—a masterpiece of what is known as the "third style" of Roman wall painting, which was popular during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful take on architectural design. A low red base (called a dado) runs along the bottom of the wall, and from it rise thin white columns against a black background. These nearly weightless columns support small pavilions, candelabras, tripods, and a narrow ledge that runs around the room, all decorated with jewel-like details.

On the back wall, tiny swans—the bird of Apollo, Augustus’s patron god—perch improbably on delicate spiral lines. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style designs would have reminded viewers of Egypt’s recent annexation after Cleopatra’s death in 30 B.C. This architectural arrangement creates almost no sense of depth or solidity. The black walls behind seem both flat and to dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Polished to a high shine, these walls must have seemed magical when lit by lamps at night.

NameOfTheObject: Wall painting: Perseus and Andromeda in landscape, from the imperial villa at Boscotrecase
CuratorialText_x:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.

NameOfTheObject: Marble stele (grave marker) of a woman
CuratorialText_x: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.

Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.

CuratorialText_x: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.

On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.

NameOfTheObject: Marble sarcophagus with the Triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons
CuratorialText_x: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.

NameOfTheObject: erracotta volute-krater (bowl for mixing wine and water)
CuratorialText_x:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.

The ancient Greeks almost never showed real events or historical people in their art. Even though writers in the 400s B.C. made it clear that the Greeks knew how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists did not try to picture those major events or famous people. Instead, they preferred to show grand, mythical battles between Greeks and enemies from the east, especially the Amazons.

The Amazons were a legendary race of warrior women. Their homeland was far to the east and north. In Athens, during the first half of the 400s B.C., the most famous Amazon battles were shown in large wall paintings. These paintings decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). These artworks had a big influence. That influence shows in the scene on this vase and on the nearby krater, number 06.286.86.

The ancient Greeks almost never depicted real historical events or people in their art. Even though writings from the fifth century B.C. show that the Greeks understood how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists did not try to illustrate those major events or famous figures. Instead, they preferred to show grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern enemies, especially the Amazons.

In Athens during the first half of the fifth century B.C., the most famous Amazon battle scenes were large wall paintings that decorated two important buildings: the Theseion (a temple dedicated to the hero Theseus) and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico, a public building with famous murals). These paintings had a major influence on later art, and their style and subject matter can be seen in this vase and in the nearby krater (a large mixing bowl for wine), numbered 06.286.86.

CuratorialText_x: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.

The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects arranged together. Flemish painters started making these in the 1500s.

A still life might include shells, books, food, drinks, cups, skulls, or dead animals. It can also show flowers, insects, or even a person in the background. In these paintings, life seems to stop for a moment.

Soon, artists began to focus on different kinds of still lifes. For example, Rachel Ruysch mainly painted flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food, cups, and plates.

Another type is the vanitas still life. It often shows a skull. This reminds viewers that life is short and that material things do not last forever.

Within this category, artists soon developed their own areas of focus. For example, Rachel Ruysch specialized in painting flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda were known for creating compositions with food, drinking vessels, and utensils. Another type, the vanitas still life, often includes a skull and is meant to remind viewers of the fleeting nature of life and the material world.

CuratorialText_x: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.

CuratorialText_x: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.

Many garments were made for special events. Others show how technology changed the way clothes were mass-produced. CuratorialText_x: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.

CuratorialText_x: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText_x: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.

CuratorialText_x: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.

CuratorialText_x: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.

CuratorialText_x: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.

CuratorialText_x: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.

CuratorialText_x: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.

CuratorialText_x: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText_x: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.

CuratorialText_x: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.

CuratorialText_x: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.

CuratorialText_x: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.

CuratorialText_x: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.

He skillfully played England and France, the Republic's main enemies, against each other. Despite this, in 1672, these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attacked the Netherlands. Johan’s brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw military operations as a representative of the States General, the national assembly. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Long after their deaths, they are remembered as champions of what was called 'true freedom'—a political ideal that emphasized civic leadership over monarchy.

CuratorialText_x: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
