CuratorialText_x: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText_x: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText_x: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.

CuratorialText_x:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText_x: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText_x: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.

CuratorialText_x: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.

CuratorialText_x: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.

CuratorialText_x: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText_x: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

The display compares the Canaanites with other groups. These groups are the Amorites and the Aramaeans from central inland Syria.

These items are presented alongside the major historical events that shaped this long period. The objects on display reveal how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant (an area that includes modern-day Israel, Palestine, Jordan, and Lebanon) remained remarkably consistent over thousands of years. They also highlight the local, indigenous roots of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The exhibition draws a comparison between this culture and that of the people from central inland Syria—specifically, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText_x: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.

Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.

Iron Age artifacts from Urartu include winged bulls and griffins—mythical creatures that were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText_x: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition looked back more than 2,000 years. It explored the origins of sacred art in three major religions: Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. This art began with ancient nature spirits in India. These spirits were seen as powerful forces in the natural world.

The exhibition was one of the first of its kind. It examined early devotional art from India through a multi-faith, modern, and global lens. It highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar images of gods and enlightened teachers from these world religions. It also showed how these images spread beyond India. They traveled across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia. They also moved along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

It traced how these traditions drew inspiration from the ancient, powerful nature spirits of India, and how this art later spread beyond the subcontinent. As one of the first major exhibitions worldwide to examine early devotional art in India from a multi-faith, contemporary, and global perspective, it highlighted the sources of inspiration behind the now-familiar images of deities and enlightened teachers in these world religions. The exhibition also showed how these artistic traditions were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText_x: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.

It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.

CuratorialText_x: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.

Hindu art included cosmic serpents. Jain art featured nature spirits that attend enlightened teachers. Together, these artworks tell powerful stories.
persona2_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that came before images of the Buddha in human form, to the cosmic serpents included in Hindu art, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers—this powerful exhibition presented the ancient stories behind these living traditions.

CuratorialText_x: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.

Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.

Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was born during a troubled time in Japan's history. He became one of the country's most talented, productive, and popular artists. As Japan faced pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige's calm art connected with people from all levels of society. His work gave them a sense of comfort.

Hiroshige captured many sides of life in his time. He showed fashionable people and lively city scenes. He also painted remote landscapes and impressions of nature. His beautiful bird-and-flower prints reveal a poetic feeling for the natural world. His landscapes reflected a growing interest in travel across Japan.

Hiroshige sometimes showed his world exactly as it was. But often, he painted it the way he imagined it could be.

His work captured many facets of life in his time, from fashionable figures and lively city scenes to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world. Stunning prints of birds and flowers reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect a growing fascination with travel across Japan. CuratorialText_x: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.

Besides his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held fans. These fans were cheap and available to everyone.

His work was also affordable: in addition to his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to everyone.

CuratorialText_x: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.

A lion and a bull named Nandi stand nearby. Many attendants surround the gods. Some attendants fly in the air. Others stand on the ground. They are musicians and garland-bearers.

A row of ritual items appears below. These include two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText_x: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
CuratorialText_y: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona1_adapted:
This is a statue of the head and upper body of Ramses II. It is made of pink and grey granite. It was one of a pair placed in front of the door of the Ramesseum, his memorial temple.

The king wears a royal headdress called a *nemes*. He also wears a circlet of uraei—that is, a crown decorated with cobras. About half of the cobras are now missing.

The sculptor used the two colors of the stone on purpose. The pink and grey help to separate the king’s face from his body.

On the back pillar, there are vertical rows of hieroglyphs. These carvings give the king’s name and titles. They also include part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noted traces of color on the statue. It may have been painted red in ancient times.
persona2_adapted: Head and upper body of a pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair originally placed before the door of the Ramesseum). The statue wears a *nemes* head-cloth and a circlet of *uraei* (about half of this is now lost). The sculptor used the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasize the division between body and face. The dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs. These give the name and titles of the king, as well as part of a dedication to Amun-Ra. In 1817, it was noted that there were traces of color on the statue. Therefore, it may have been painted red in antiquity.
persona3_adapted: Head and upper body of a monumental statue of Ramses II, carved from pink and grey granite. This statue was originally one of a pair placed in front of the entrance to the Ramesseum, the king’s mortuary temple. The king wears the traditional striped headcloth, known as a nemes, and a circlet of sacred cobras (uraei), though about half of this crown is now missing. The sculptor made use of the stone’s two-tone coloring to create a clear visual separation between the body and the face. On the back pillar, vertical columns of hieroglyphs record the king’s name and titles, along with part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, observers noted traces of color on the statue, suggesting that it may originally have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText_x: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.

The bottom and sides of the neck cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

At the front of the crown, a crest rises up. It has a cone-shaped roundel with a central bump, surrounded by seven rings. These rings probably represent beads. Behind the roundel, a braided element rises up and ends in a pointed, egg-shaped tip. Both parts have traces of black paint.

The face is slightly long and has vertical carved lines (called striations). Two lines of holes run between the earlobes. One line goes through the angle of the neck and jaw. The other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip.

The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the head. The eyebrow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not carved with lines. There are grooves around the front of the neck that represent skin creases. Holes are pierced through the neck at the front and both sides. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.

On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, you can see two claws, each with bird legs along the edges.

On the dart-shaped spaces on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third darts. There are also traces of red and blue paint.

The light, crystalline marble is fairly smooth on the surface, but the underside is broken and rough.

CuratorialText_x: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
CuratorialText_y: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona1_adapted:
This is a small Egyptian decoration made from baked clay (terracotta). It is shaped like a palm leaf fan (a palmette) and was once the top corner of a tiny altar.

The altar was made using a mold, likely in two or more pieces. You can still see traces of a dull red coating (slip) on the inside and outside. There are also signs of burning inside.

The clay is brown Nile silt with a light-brown center. It contains lots of tiny gold mica flakes and some white bits.
persona2_adapted: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner was broken from a miniature altar. Each side is decorated with a half-palmette. The altar was made using a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but possibly more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, both inside and out. There are also signs of burning within. The material is brown Nile silt with a light-brown core. It contains abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona3_adapted: This small decorative piece, made of Egyptian terracotta, was originally the upper corner of a miniature altar. It is shaped like a palmette—a fan-like ornament based on palm leaves, a common motif in ancient art. The altar from which it came was mass-produced using a mold, likely made in two parts, though possibly more. Traces of a matte red coating, called a slip, remain on both the inside and outside. There are also signs of burning inside, suggesting it was used in rituals involving fire. The clay is brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, and it contains abundant fine gold mica along with some white inclusions.

CuratorialText_x: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
CuratorialText_y: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted:
This cloth is called *adire eleko*. It is a type of indigo-dyed fabric. The dye was applied using a resist method, where parts of the cloth are protected from the dye to create patterns.

The cloth is made from factory-made cotton. Two panels of the fabric were sewn together by machine. Each panel is divided into a grid of four squares by seven squares.

The patterns were painted by hand. Two of the squares show the pillars of Mapo Hall, a famous building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that look like pestles (or spoons).
persona2_adapted: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine-sewn together. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan). Between these pillars are four pestles (or spoons).
persona3_adapted: This cloth is a type of indigo-dyed fabric known as *adire eleko*, made using a resist-dyeing technique. It is sewn from two machine-stitched panels of factory-made cotton. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows by seven squares. The patterns are hand-painted, and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall (a landmark building in Ibadan, Nigeria). Between these pillars are four shapes that could represent pestles or spoons.

A manuscript of his famous novel *Robinson Crusoe* sticks out of his pocket. Defoe’s worried wife and daughter meet him as he leaves. Books and an artist’s palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. The image was made after an earlier design by the artist Ward, in 1851.

The paper used here is a thin sheet glued onto a stronger backing, a technique called *chine collé*.

CuratorialText_x: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.

persona3_adapted: In this oil painting on canvas, the central scene depicts the worship of the Śālagrāma—a sacred stone believed to embody the presence of the god Vishnu. On the left, the child god Krishna (referred to here as Balakṛṣṇa) is shown holding a ball of butter. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne, suggesting a form of reverence that avoids directly depicting Vishnu’s image—a practice known as aniconic worship. This idea is reinforced by the large conch shell placed prominently in the foreground, a symbol closely associated with Vishnu. Other ritual objects commonly used in Hindu worship, or *puja*, are also visible, including a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText_x: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.

persona1_adapted:
This is a portrait of Lady Layard, the wife of Henry Layard. It is an oil painting on canvas, set in a carved and gold-covered wooden frame.

In the painting, Lady Layard sits on the balcony of Ca Capello. That was the Layards' house in Venice, Italy. The balcony looks over the Grand Canal, a famous waterway.

She wears a tight red jacket with a belt and a long black skirt. The skirt has a very long train that trails behind her. She holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony railing.

Behind her, open French doors with glass panels lead inside. You can see potted plants in the room. In the distance, there is a view across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText_x: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
CuratorialText_y: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This small pottery container, called an alabastron, was made in ancient Corinth. It was shaped on a potter's wheel. The body is like an egg with a flat bottom. It has a small mouth with a sloping rim and a small handle at the top for hanging.

The pot is made of fine, light-colored clay with a thin coating called slip. The painted decoration turned black when fired in the kiln. The artist also added purple paint on the animals and carved fine details into the clay.

The main band around the pot shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion. Between them is a tree-like design. A lion fills the space between the two griffins. Small decorative shapes fill the empty areas.

Above the main scene is a band of flower-like designs. Below are simpler patterns made of dots separated by S-shaped lines. Near the bottom are simple lines like rays. On the lip of the vase are tongue-shaped decorations.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This Protocorinthian alabastron is made of pottery. It is wheel-made. The body is depressed-ovoid and sits on a rounded base. The mouth is small with a sloping rim. At the top, there is a pierced lug handle. The vase is made of fine buff clay and slip. The painted decoration has fired black. Purple paint was added on the animal bodies. Details of the figures are incised.

The main frieze shows two griffins. A stylized tree-like motif separates them. A lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts. There are filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylized floral designs. Below are simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays appear on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona3_adapted:
This small pottery container, known as an alabastron, was made in the ancient Greek region of Corinth. It was shaped on a potter's wheel, with a rounded, egg-like body, a narrow mouth with a sloping rim, and a small handle at the top. The vessel is made from fine, light-colored clay and was coated with a thin liquid clay called slip. The painted decoration, which turned black during firing, covers much of the surface. The artist also added purple paint to the bodies of the animals, and carved fine details into the figures.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle—separated by a stylized, tree-like design. A lion fills the space between these two mythical beasts. Around them are smaller, decorative shapes that fill the empty background. Above this main scene is a band of stylized floral patterns, and below it, simpler motifs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase itself is decorated with simple radiating lines, and the lip of the vessel has a pattern of tongue-like shapes.

CuratorialText_x: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.

CuratorialText_x:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.

It is a valuable resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection keeps growing. It now has over 25,000 hours of moving images. persona3_adapted:
IWM has been managing a Film Archive for over a hundred years. From the very beginning, the institution recognized that film could serve as a vital primary source—an original record of events, not just entertainment. This extensive collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict, from the First World War to the present day. It is a remarkable resource for researchers and filmmakers alike.

Our constantly growing collection now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide and diverse range of material: public information films, service films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access, with newly-digitized media added as soon as it becomes available.

persona1_adapted:
This painting shows the sinking of HMS *Laurentic*. In the background, half of the ship is underwater. Its bow (the front) sticks up into the air. In the front, a lifeboat full of sailors glows in the dark from a small light. Another lifeboat is on the right. Tiny lights in the distance show more lifeboats. On the left, you can see the top of a German U-boat (a submarine).

HMS *Laurentic* was first a passenger liner for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, the navy added 6-inch guns and turned it into an armed merchant cruiser. This was the first ship for the artist, Frank Worsley. He stayed with it until a German U-boat captain, Otto Kretschmer, sank it off the coast of Ulster on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the first version of this scene right after the attack, while on leave. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought that painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America for a traveling war art show. But the ship carrying it was torpedoed (hit by a torpedo) and the painting was lost. So, the WAAC asked Worsley to paint this replacement.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The image shows HMS *Laurentic* sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its bow rises above the surface. In the foreground, a lifeboat full of sailors is illuminated against the darkness by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right. Small dots of light in the background indicate more lifeboats. On the left side of the background, the top of a German U-boat can be seen.

HMS *Laurentic* was originally a P&O liner. In 1939, it was equipped with 6-inch guns and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. This was Worsley’s first ship. He remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster, by U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately afterward, while on survivors’ leave. The WAAC (War Artists' Advisory Committee) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a touring exhibition of war art. However, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed en route. At the request of the WAAC, the present picture was painted as a replacement.

(Source: Exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona3_adapted:
On the night of November 3, 1940, the British ship HMS Laurentic was sunk off the coast of Ulster by a German U-boat commanded by Otto Kretschmer. In this painting, we see the ship in the background, half submerged, with its bow rising out of the water. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit by a small light against the darkness. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny points of light in the distance suggest more lifeboats. On the left, the top of the German submarine is visible.

HMS Laurentic began its life as a passenger liner for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into what was called an armed merchant cruiser—a civilian ship repurposed for military use. The artist, Frank Worsley, served on the Laurentic as his first ship, and he stayed with it until it was sunk. He painted the original version of this scene immediately after the attack, while on leave as a survivor.

The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC), a British government agency that commissioned and collected art related to the war, bought that first painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. But the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed, and the artwork was lost. At the WAAC’s request, Worsley painted this replacement version.

(Adapted from exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

NameOfTheObject: Mildmay Grove looking to the South West
CuratorialText_x: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' persona1_adapted:
**The Artwork and Its Story**

This drawing shows a view across a road with trees. On the other side is a high wall next to a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti that says "Nuclear state = Police state." Next to it is an anarchy symbol and the words "One World." Behind the wall on the right is a large factory building. Further right is a road junction with another road leading off to the left.

**What the Artist Said**

In the summer of 1983, artist Eric Rimmington found an old train station in Mildmay Grove. He started making ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized something important. The train line he was drawing was used several times a week to carry nuclear waste. The waste came from Sizewell nuclear power station and went to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel processing plant. Over the next six weeks, Rimmington could not stop thinking about this. He said it became part of every mark he made in his drawings.

**The Historical Context**

Rimmington made these drawings during a time of strong anti-nuclear protests. People were protesting against both nuclear weapons and nuclear power plants. In 1979, the British Government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be placed in the UK. This decision sparked many marches and protests. One famous protest was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which began in 1981. It was at its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the base in Berkshire.

Also in 1979, an accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more worried about the safety of nuclear power.

**Why It Still Matters**

Nuclear waste, and how it is moved and stored, is still a concern today. People continue to protest about it. For example, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly asked for a report on how safe nuclear waste trains are.

NameOfTheObject: Evacuees in a Cottage at Cookham Children in Wartime - Five lithographs by Ethel Gabain
CuratorialText_x:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).

**Label**
This is Mrs. Norris of Cookham. She is with five of her "war family"—seven children from London. By January 1941, these children had been with her for sixteen months. persona2_adapted:
**Object Description**
Five children sit around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the center, holding the youngest child on her lap. A large "Spotted-Dick" pudding is placed on the table, with several bowls and cutlery arranged around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right, who holds out a bowl for his portion.

**Label**
"Mrs. Norris, from Cookham, is shown with five of her 'war family'—seven children from London who, by January 1941, had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris represents the ideal foster parent, and there are many other householders like her. More than one million children have been moved in school groups from evacuation areas. With such large numbers, placing children in private homes (called 'billets') must remain the main method of finding homes for them. Because of Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, city children quickly adapt to their new surroundings." (Title from folder cover: *Children in Wartime*).

**Notes on Cultural References**
- **Spotted-Dick pudding**: A traditional British steamed dessert made with suet and dried fruit (often currants or raisins). The name is a colloquial term; the dish itself is a common, homely food.
- **War family**: Refers to children evacuated from cities during World War II to live with host families in safer rural areas.
- **Billets**: A military term used here to mean private homes where evacuees were temporarily housed.

**Label**

This photograph shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham, along with five of the seven children from London who had been evacuated to her home during the war. By January 1941, they had been living with her for sixteen months. The original caption describes Mrs. Norris as the ideal foster parent, and notes that many other householders like her took in evacuated children. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas as part of school-organized relocations. With such large numbers, the main way to house them was to place them with local families in private homes. The caption suggests that, given Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, city children readily adapted to their new surroundings.

persona3_adapted:
In this portrait, we see a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a lathe—a machine used for shaping metal. She had been identified by the War Artist’s Advisory Committee as an especially skilled factory worker. The artist, Laura Knight, originally planned to paint a formal studio portrait, but the Ministry of Supply asked that Loftus be shown at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. There, she was making a Bofors breech ring, a part for an anti-aircraft gun. This was considered the most demanding job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years of training. Loftus was just 21 at the time and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine so quickly was a major public relations success for the war effort—though some have suggested she may have been placed at the machine precisely to create that impression.

Industrial machinery was a completely new subject for Knight, but contemporary reports praised the technical accuracy of her painting. In this way, Knight—like Loftus—was proving herself in a field traditionally dominated by men. Knight was known for her fascination with circus performers and dancers, and here she emphasizes the balance and posture of her subject while working. The painting captures not just a worker at a machine, but a woman who had mastered complex engineering skills in a remarkably short time, and an artist who rose to the challenge of depicting an unfamiliar world with precision and insight.

image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.

CuratorialText_x:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.

In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. persona1_adapted:
On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—sailed near the coast of Turkey. Their destination was Leros, a Greek island held by British forces. Aboard the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking officer on his way to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships also carried soldiers, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* hit a mine—an underwater explosive—between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship blew up and sank in just two minutes. Besides its own crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only around 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF air-sea rescue boat from Leros and by boats from HMS *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He wrote about this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.
persona2_adapted:
On 23 October 1943, two destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—sailed along the Turkish coast. Their destination was Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands. At that time, a British garrison occupied Leros. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East. He was traveling to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships also carried troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 PM, the *Eclipse* struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in only two minutes. In addition to its own crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* carried another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only about 100 men survived. They were rescued by an air-sea rescue launch from the RAF, dispatched from Leros, and by boats sent from HMS *Petard*. The survivors had spent over three hours in the water.

Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, and retired from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it occurred.
persona3_adapted:
On October 23, 1943, two British Royal Navy destroyers—the HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the Turkish coast toward the Greek island of Leros, which was then held by a British garrison. Aboard the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations for the Middle East, who was traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* struck a mine in the sea between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion tore the ship apart, and it sank in just two minutes. In addition to its regular crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company from the Buffs (a British infantry regiment). Only around 100 men survived. They were rescued by an air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from the *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText_x: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.

Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol. She returned there during World War II after living in Paris for over 10 years as an artist. She never got an official job as a war artist. This was because of her left-wing political views and her abstract style. But with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This drawing shows an abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders in various sizes. It explores how bombs distort building structures. The rhythm and form of the girders make the violence of the blast visible.

Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol. She returned there during the Second World War, after spending more than 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never received an official commission as a war artist. This was due to her left-wing political associations and the abstract nature of her work. However, with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she obtained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona3_adapted:
This drawing shows a tangle of twisted metal beams, coiled and bent into an abstract shape. It captures how bombs distorted the structures of buildings during wartime. The violent force of the explosion is visible in the twisting forms and uneven rhythm of the girders.

The artist, Paule Vézelay, was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War, after spending more than a decade working as an artist in Paris. She never received an official commission as a war artist—likely because of her left-wing political views and the abstract, non-realistic style of her work. However, with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she obtained a permit to draw the city's bomb-damaged ruins.

CuratorialText_x: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText_x: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.

He named the large building he found there the "Palace of Minos," after the legendary king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. According to the myth, the Labyrinth was a cleverly designed maze that King Minos ordered built to imprison the terrifying Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the story, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, a famous hero who was part human and part divine. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was later regarded as the founder of Athens. These Cretan myths originally came from stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told long after the Palace itself had fallen into ruin.

CuratorialText_x:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText_x:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.

NameOfTheObject: View of the High Street, Oxford
CuratorialText_x: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.

persona2_adapted:
The painting originated from an important early commission. Turner was asked to create two watercolours for engraving. These were intended for the annual calendar published by the University Press, known as the *Oxford Almanack*. The watercolours were so successful that Turner eventually completed ten of them between 1798 and 1804.

This work, along with one other, was later executed in oil paint. The commission came from a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the view. He also introduced figures into the composition: members of the University, clergy, and some women—the latter included “for the sake of colour.”

This painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works. The reason is that Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist. This correspondence was later included in Wyatt’s posthumous sale.

The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. However, Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolour. The canvas was half the size of Turner’s usual works. The cost was 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812.

Later, in the 1830s, Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours. He chose not to include the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unmatched level of technical mastery. He believed he could not repeat that achievement.
persona3_adapted:
This painting began as part of an important early job: Turner was asked to create two watercolors that would be turned into engravings—printed illustrations—for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The works were so well received that Turner eventually completed ten watercolors for the project between 1798 and 1804. Later, this piece and one other were painted in oils—a different, richer type of paint—for a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt.

Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the scene. He also added figures: members of the university, clergy, and some women, whom he included “for the sake of colour”—meaning to add visual interest and variety to the composition. This painting is one of the best-documented works in Turner’s entire career, because Wyatt kept the letters the artist sent him, and those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original goal of the commission was to create a design that could be engraved for printing. But Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolor, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas (a significant sum at the time). The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Later, in the 1830s, when Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors, he chose not to paint the High Street again. He felt that in this painting, he had achieved a level of technical skill so remarkable that he could not surpass it.

CuratorialText_x: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.

*The Hunt in the Forest* is a very original painting. It shows a nighttime landscape with a strong, clear structure. Hunting was a fancy hobby for nobles, with its own special rules. But the idea of a hunt at night is playful. It might be symbolic, not realistic.

To create this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first drew a grid on the wooden panel. He placed a single vanishing point in the center. This point pulls our eyes deep into the scene. When painting, he added details like the hunters' spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front. These items help focus our attention on the space and the shrinking shapes of men and animals.

Gold specks in the tree leaves would have matched the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest. When lit by candles, the gold would sparkle and seem to move.

Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was famous in his time for his skill with perspective, animals, and landscapes. His nickname, Uccello (which means "Bird"), refers to his art of the natural world. He was a versatile designer who also worked on mosaics and stained glass. He combined all these skills to create this masterpiece.

*The Hunt* is a type of painting called a *spalliera* (spah-lee-AIR-ah). This means it was mounted at shoulder height. It could be the backboard of a decorated chest or set into a room's wooden panels. We don't know who first bought it, but it was clearly made for a Renaissance palace. It might have been in Urbino, where Uccello worked from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText_x: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.

NameOfTheObject: Still Life with a Faience Figure
CuratorialText_x: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

NameOfTheObject: St Nicholas of Bari banishing the Storm
CuratorialText_x: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
CuratorialText_y: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona1_adapted:
This panel was once part of a larger artwork called an altarpiece. An altarpiece is a painting or sculpture placed behind a church altar. This one was made between 1433 and 1435 for a church in Florence, Italy. Today, the different parts of the altarpiece are spread across many museums.

The center of the altarpiece showed the Virgin Mary, baby Jesus, and four angels. On each side were pairs of saints, including Saint Benedict and Saint Nicholas. The bottom section, called the predella, showed scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these scenes still exist today. They include his birth, pilgrims at his shrine, and him saving three young people. Another scene shows him giving money to three sisters, which is why three gold balls are his symbol. A final scene shows him calming a storm.

The panels probably had frames shaped like four-leaf clovers, called quatrefoils. The artist, Bicci, based his design on an earlier altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano.
persona2_adapted: This panel comes from the predella of a large altarpiece (1433–35) made for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. The parts of this altarpiece are now in different collections. In the center was the Virgin and Child with Four Angels. On each side were pairs of saints: Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari, and St John the Baptist with an Evangelist, probably St Matthew. The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas. Five of these scenes are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the saint’s shrine; St Nicholas bringing three youths back to life; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and calming a storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano’s Quaratesi altarpiece of 1425, made for S. Niccolò oltr’Arno.
persona3_adapted: This panel was originally part of the lower section (called the *predella*) of a large altarpiece created between 1433 and 1435 for the church of San Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. Today, the altarpiece’s various parts are scattered across different collections. At the center of the altarpiece was a scene of the Virgin and Child with Four Angels, flanked on either side by pairs of saints: Saints Benedict and Nicholas of Bari on one side, and Saint John the Baptist with an Evangelist (likely Saint Matthew) on the other. The *predella* depicted scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas, five of which are still known: his birth; pilgrims visiting his shrine; the saint bringing three youths back to life; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (shown as three gold balls, his traditional symbol); and his calming of a storm. The panels probably originally had frames shaped like four-leaf clovers (called *quatrefoils*). The artist, Bicci, based the design of this panel on an earlier altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano—the Quaratesi altarpiece of 1425, made for the church of San Niccolò oltr'Arno.

NameOfTheObject: Manzai, or strolling musicians, celebrating New Year, with poem on the reverse
Medium: painting with ink and colour on silk; calligraphy with ink on paper; painting and calligraphy framed with silver paper
CuratorialText_x: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.

CuratorialText_x: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.

Hughes used small details to make the scene more emotional. Spiderwebs, dew drops, dog roses, and dandelion seeds all remind us that life is brief and things do not last. The boy's loss is also shown by a lamb separated from its mother by the tomb.

The boy's sorrow is echoed in the image of a lamb separated from its mother by the stone barrier of the tomb.

NameOfTheObject: A Woman Seated at the Piano
CuratorialText_x: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.

NameOfTheObject: The Feast in the House of Levi
CuratorialText_x: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
CuratorialText_y: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona1_adapted: In 1573, the artist Veronese painted a famous version of the Last Supper for a dining hall in Venice. This painting got him in trouble with the Inquisition, a religious court. They accused him of blasphemy, which means showing disrespect to God or religion. To fix the problem, Veronese changed the painting's title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*. This new subject allowed him to include many colorful details, based on a Bible story (Luke 6:27-30). Many copies of this large painting exist. The original is now in the Accademia museum in Venice. WA1855.159 is a smaller, high-quality copy. It was made in the mid-1700s by a Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

Many copies of this large painting exist; the original is now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of high quality, made by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

To avoid controversy, he changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*, a biblical scene that allowed for more lively and colorful details (based on Luke 6:27–30). Many copies of this large work, now housed in the Accademia museum in Venice, exist. The painting WA1855.159 is a smaller, high-quality version made by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, likely Francesco Fontebasso.
