persona1_adapted:
For over 50 years, people have studied and collected pottery from the James River Valley. Before the Civil War, more than a dozen potteries operated along the James River and its smaller branches. Potters came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England. They brought their own regional styles and created a rich, mixed tradition. They shaped their pots from local clays, dug from places like Kaolin and Claymount.

These pots are more than just useful objects. Many are on display here for the first time. They give us a remarkable look at a long-lasting pottery industry in eastern Virginia. When seen together, they show a bold decorative style. This style is just as impressive as other famous pottery traditions from Virginia and the rest of the country.

Today, many of these historic sites are at risk from new development. Luckily, archaeologists and decorative arts experts are working ahead of the bulldozers. They are documenting and studying the important legacy of the James River Potteries before it is lost forever. At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a long article about these stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ and Oliver Mueller-Heubach.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText_x:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.

The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition presents objects made by these craftspeople, whose origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it links those objects to the makers’ own words—through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these materials tell the story of journeys taken, communities formed in new homes, responses to shifting political conditions, and the creative fusion of traditions into something new.

An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastelist—an artist who worked with pastel chalks—from several different countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. What ties these individuals together is that they were all “lately from” somewhere else, having recently arrived in the American South during the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition displays objects made by these skilled workers, whose origins spanned continents and oceans. Wherever possible, it links those objects to the makers’ own words—through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these items and writings tell a story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to shifting political climates, and the creative mixing of traditions to form something entirely new.

CuratorialText_x: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.

CuratorialText_x: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText_x:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.

CuratorialText_x:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time

CuratorialText_x: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.

CuratorialText_x: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText_x: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
CuratorialText_y: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
persona1_adapted:
For over forty years, the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected some of the finest examples of furniture made in the American South. These objects show how the early South changed over time. They also tell us about the many different people who came to this region. And they help us learn about the skilled workers, or artisans, who were an important part of that story.

These furniture makers came from many places. They were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French. They used the materials they had nearby, as well as materials brought from other countries. The furniture they created tells us about their different backgrounds and the many sides of their daily lives. These craftspeople helped create a blend of art, culture, and history. That blend is the foundation for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers used the materials available to them—both local and imported—to create furniture. This furniture speaks to us about their varied backgrounds and the many different aspects of their daily lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic combination of art, culture, and history that forms the foundation of MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

These craftspeople contributed to a rich blend of art, culture, and history that remains the foundation of MESDA’s ongoing research into the early South.

CuratorialText_x: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText_x: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.

Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.

Brass bands also played often during religious services. They even used music to announce births and deaths in the community. The Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection includes musical instruments and related items. These objects show how important music was to the community. Other items in the collection highlight musical craftsmanship from other parts of the American South.

Other objects in the collection highlight the skill of musical craftsmanship found elsewhere across the American South.

CuratorialText_x: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings

However, MESDA's painting collection shows a wide range of training and styles. It includes works by artists trained in Europe and artists born in America. These artists lived in both cities and rural areas.

CuratorialText_x: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
